steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Build a React single-page Hashi (Bridges) puzzle game with a retro terminal look: black background, green monospace text, no CSS frameworks. Source goes under /app/src/, served on http://localhost:3000.

Islands are numbered circles with values 1–8; the number is the exact bridge count required at that island. Bridges are straight horizontal or vertical lines between two islands, max two per pair, never crossing. The puzzle is solved when every island's count is satisfied and all islands form one connected component.

clicking island A then island B cycles the bridge between them through none → single → double → none. Right-click or Ctrl+click on a bridge toggles it. arrow keys move a visible cursor between islands; Enter selects an island, arrow keys move toward another, Enter toggles the bridge; Esc cancels. Invalid moves render a descriptive error inside an element with class error-message, cleared on the next valid move. The error text must be one of: "Cannot connect non-adjacent islands", "Max 2 bridges per pair", "Island would exceed its number", "Bridges would cross".

Puzzle data lives at /app/src/data/puzzles.js with three puzzles labeled Easy, Medium, and Hard, selectable via a dropdown. A Reset button clears all bridges on the current puzzle while leaving the islands in place. When the puzzle is solved, render a banner with class victory-banner containing the text SOLVED!.

The board is a single SVG of 800×600 anchored to the viewport top-left — no centering, no page padding. Each grid unit is 40px, so an island at grid (x, y) renders at pixel (x*40, y*40). Islands are SVG circle elements; bridges are line elements (two parallel lines for a double). The keyboard cursor must be clearly visible but must not be a circle — use rect, stroke filter, group transform, anything else — so the board's circle count always equals the island count.

Game logic lives at /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js. Bridges are stored as Map<string, number> keyed by the canonical "x1,y1,x2,y2" string where the endpoint with the smaller (x, then y) sorts first; coordinates in the key are grid units. canAddBridge(islands, bridges, from, to, delta) returning { valid, error } where error, when non-null, is one of the four strings above; computeCurrentCounts(islands, bridges) returning Map<islandId, number>; checkConnectivity(islands, bridges) returning whether every island is reachable from every other; isVictory(islands, bridges) returning whether every island's count equals its value, no bridges cross, and the graph is connected.

One thing worth flagging from prototyping: the error-message and victory-banner only need to exist while their condition is active, and the dropdown handler should fully reset bridges when the puzzle changes. The first mouse click on an island is just selection — the toggle runs on the second click against a different island.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@72bd201bd497:/app#

Analysis: We are at /app with no prior command output shown. The React app source and required files (/app/src/data/puzzles.js, /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js, and UI components/styles) have not been created/verified yet. Next step is to inspect the existing project structure under /app/src and then implement the puzzle data, game logic utilities, and the React UI (SVG board, selection/toggling, keyboard cursor, error/victory banners, reset and puzzle dropdown).
Plan: 1) Inspect the current repository (package.json, src structure) to confirm it’s a React app and see existing files. 2) Create/overwrite /app/src/data/puzzles.js with Easy/Medium/Hard puzzles. 3) Create/overwrite /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js implementing canonical bridge keys, crossing checks, validation, counts, connectivity, victory. 4) Implement the React UI in /app/src/App.js (or relevant entry) to render an 800x600 SVG at top-left with retro styling, handle mouse and keyboard interactions, error handling, reset, puzzle selection, and victory banner. 5) Add minimal CSS (no frameworks) for black background and green monospace. 6) Run tests via npm start/build to ensure it serves on localhost:3000 and meets requirements.

Analysis: This is a Vite + React project, but the expected /app/src directory does not exist yet. We need to create the full React app source under /app/src (main entry, App, styles) plus the required /app/src/data/puzzles.js and /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js, and ensure Vite can serve it on localhost:3000.
Plan: 1) Inspect the rest of the repo (vite config, index.html) to see what’s currently present/missing. 2) Create the missing Vite/React standard files: index.html (if missing), src/main.jsx, src/App.jsx, src/styles.css. 3) Add puzzles at src/data/puzzles.js. 4) Implement all game logic at src/utils/hashiLogic.js. 5) Wire up the UI in App.jsx: dropdown, reset, error-message, victory-banner, SVG rendering, mouse interactions, keyboard cursor and navigation, and bridge toggling with validations. 6) Run `npm run dev -- --host 0.0.0.0 --port 3000` to confirm it serves correctly.

steps.message:
Analysis: The repository only contains package.json/lock and node_modules; there is no Vite scaffolding (no index.html, no vite.config, no src/). We must create a minimal Vite+React project structure ourselves under /app, ensuring all required code lives under /app/src and can be served on localhost:3000.
Plan: 1) Create index.html, vite.config.js, and src/ tree (main.jsx, App.jsx, styles.css). 2) Add required puzzle data at src/data/puzzles.js. 3) Implement hashi game logic at src/utils/hashiLogic.js (canonical bridge keys, adjacency, crossing, validation, counts, connectivity, victory). 4) Implement UI and interactions in App.jsx: SVG board 800x600 anchored top-left, islands as circles, bridges as lines, mouse selection and toggling rules, right-click/Ctrl-click toggle on bridges, keyboard cursor (rect/marker), arrow navigation between islands, Enter selection/toggle, Esc cancel, error-message and victory-banner conditional rendering, dropdown puzzle selection resets bridges, Reset button clears bridges. 5) Run dev server on port 3000.

steps.message:
Analysis: Project scaffold and source files were created (index.html, vite.config.js, src/* including puzzles and hashiLogic, and App). `vite build` has started and is currently transforming modules; we need to wait for build completion and fix any compile/runtime errors. After a successful build, we should start the dev server on port 3000 and do a quick static check that required elements/classes exist and interactions are wired. One likely missing item: Ctrl+click on bridges is implemented, right-click is implemented; but the spec also says right-click or Ctrl+click on a bridge toggles it, and first click on island is selection only (implemented). Error messages must be one of four exact strings (implemented via ERRORS). Need to confirm build success.
Plan: 1) Wait for `npm run build` to finish and review any errors. 2) If build succeeds, run `npm run dev -- --host 0.0.0.0 --port 3000` to serve at localhost:3000. 3) If build fails, inspect the error and patch the relevant file(s), then rebuild.

Analysis: Vite dev server is running and ready at http://localhost:3000/. The app should now be accessible. Remaining work is to sanity-check requirements against the implementation and make any necessary adjustments: ensure error-message/victory-banner only render when active (they do), dropdown resets bridges (it does), board SVG is 800x600 top-left (it is), cursor is not a circle (rect is used), islands are circles (yes). One potential mismatch: right-click or Ctrl+click is specified for a bridge (we implemented on existing bridge lines only). Also ensure invalid move errors are cleared on the next valid move (we clear on valid toggles). Next: do a quick code grep for required class names and strings to confirm exact matches.
Plan: Run quick greps to confirm the four error strings are exact and that error-message and victory-banner classes exist. Confirm puzzles file location and exports. No need to stop the dev server; run commands in a separate shell by using tmux? Not available, so we'll just inspect files from this terminal while server runs only if it supports (it will keep running). We'll send Ctrl+C to stop server temporarily, run checks, then restart dev server if needed.
